speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number 2 with Rosette Fischer. Go on.

speaker: interviewee
text: Can I tell you now about-- so, one day, we heard, that was towards the liberation because we tried to get the news as much as we could. And they will take us from one camp to another.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from one camp to another?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was not far. That was from B camp to C camp. Over there, we almost didn't work. We just waiting. There was, like, a transaction camp. People use to come. As a matter of fact, I heard about my-- that my sister-- that people from Lodz were coming. I went out to, to see if she's there. But she wasn't. Instead, I saw my-- two uncles of mine passing by. And I had the chance to approach. I just ran out. I didn't care what's going to happen to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, [SOBBING] where is everybody? Where is everybody? What happened? They were from Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they told me, well, almost everybody's gone. But you-- your sister [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] passed here two weeks ago. And she's not here anymore. That's how I learned that my sister-- I will not see my sister soon. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, one-- after that, two weeks, I think-- we stayed two weeks in that camp. And then we started walking. That was January the 18, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then, we start walking-- from all the camps, from all the sides, we started walking. My mother got very tired. She would walk slower. So we'd be-- we were, like, in the back of everybody. And I would tell her, come, Ma. You wanted to survive. [SOBBING] Let's walk. And she said, no. You leave me here. You just continue. You're young. You can go. I don't have the strength anymore. And they were shooting from each side. If you stayed behind, they were shooting. If you went on the side, then you just-- you heard people just falling to the sides. And I was dragging [SOBBING] my mother. And I said, no, you cannot sit. You have to walk. [SOBBING] That's was me that was the strong at the time. And I would drag her towards almost the middle or whatever, so we wouldn't be at the end. And when everybody was sitting for five minutes, I didn't let even sit. [SOBBING] She was begging me. She couldn't. Finally, we arrived after three days and three nights walking. We arrived to Ravensbruck, where everybody was in a big hall. It must have been a recreation hall of some kind of a gymnastic place, school. So we all sat down on the floor to sit. My mother was so nervous that I couldn't let nobody be near her. And we had to be one on top of the other. She would rest my head-- her head on my lap. But if somebody touched her leg, she said, oh, no. Oh, no. [SOBBING] She just couldn't take it because she was overtired. [SOBBING] And so I wouldn't sleep. And I would beg and go, please, please. And they really were very nice about it. Everybody [SOB] was nice. I think we-- there were only two mothers in the whole camp, a friend of ours, which was alone, not with her, who adopted us-- my friends. She was same age as my mother. And my mother-- so that was the only two ladies that were-- not old, but they were 40. And over 40 was considered old at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed in that camp [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] only, I think, a night or two nights, something like that. From there, we start walking again and went to Ravensbruck. In Ravensbruck, I think we stayed three months. I really don't remember what we did. But one day-- What was Ravensbruck like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ravensbruck-- I didn't know Ravensbruck very well because we didn't stay too long. We were all together, you know. But then we left to Neustadt, Neustadt-Glewe. I understand there is another Neustadt. But this was Neustadt-Glewe in Germany. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one day, I was sent, with other friends of mine-- Mary-Claude, Doris, Addie-- we were sent to a train to bring bread to the camp. And we were allowed to eat there. Of course, each of us packed a lot of bread for the rest of the people that didn't come to pick up the bread. I was loaded with bread. And so were my friends. But when me arrived back in camp, they were saying, whoever has anything, just throw it out. Because, see, you're going to be punished. We start to throw out everything. But there was one piece that I could just-- couldn't throw out. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew my mother didn't have it. So I had hidden it. And they found it, of course. They found it. And they put up in the front, like, I don't remember, you know, how you call it, you know? A bench?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like a bench. We have two legs and just a bar across. They would put me on that. And they start hitting me. [SOBBING] I would bend down. And they were hitting me. I was supposed to get 25. But I don't think I got to 25. Because somehow, I don't know where I took the strength, I just ran-- and ran into the room. And everybody would cover me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was a very, you know-- oh, yeah, I forgot a very important thing. But that was in camp. That was still in Birkenau. One day, my friend came to see me. He had worked himself out to work in the kitchen, that's why, you know. He was one of the privileged people. He could buy off people to let him go and come. So he came. But one time he came to see me, he left me a piece of margarine.

speaker: interviewee
text: When he left, an SS came alone. She saw me. She said, what do you have? I gave her the margarine. She slapped my on the face that I-- I think I, I didn't know, you know, on my head-- I didn't know if I was coming or going.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then she took me to the-- to a place where we called the [NON-ENGLISH] that was in the front of the camp. There was a [SIGHS] podium where there was music. [CRYING] The girls will play for the people that march to work and from work. That same day, my mother went to work. I was left behind to see my friend. They took me there. They put me next, that everybody that comes from work should see me. And I was going to be punished to be in the special-- oh, I forgot how you call it. I'm very confused, I'm sorry. [SIGHS] Where it's a, a dark room-- where it's 4 by 4-- they told me, whatever-- I was going to be punished, severely punished.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they wanted everybody to see that I'm getting punished. It didn't matter to me that I'm going to be punished. But what matters to me-- [SOBBING] my mother will see me, that she's going to pass by. And she's going to see me. And how is she going to survive by herself? She wants to leave.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know what happened. After maybe two hours of standing there-- or I don't know. It was-- it seemed two hours. Somebody came and got me there. If it was my friend that did it, or somebody else, they took me off from there. And I was back into, into the barracks. It took 10 days for me. I had a nervous breakdown after that. I wasn't standing in the front anymore because I couldn't stand on my feet. I couldn't eat. I couldn't drink. My friends would hold me up in the back to be counted. Because every day they counted us. They were hiding me. They were doing their best. But I just couldn't. I would just-- my tears would come out. And I, I just didn't talk. And they would just start [SOBBING] crying. I couldn't swallow. I remember they'd changed the bread for something, like, I don't know, an apple or something. My mother would scrape and give it to me in my mouth. It took 10 days till I really start coming back to myself. That was a very bad experience for me. And after the camp, I had nightmares. All those nightmares, I don't know if they did something to me because I'm still taking some pills that the doctor prescribed for me after the war. [SIGHS] My mother and I survived. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know how. I didn't do, certainly, didn't do nothing to survive. Why my sister didn't survive, why others didn't survive, why the little kids that I saw marching with all the men that they told me that there's not enough gas for them, that the goal that used to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] thrown into a-- to a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to a hall-- they're going to be burned alive. There were all the people and children. This I could never understand because children that never-- never had the time to do anything wrong. If I did something wrong-- I don't know. If I did, I had age already. I was-- but a child, a child of two, a child of one, little children. This upset me always. And I'm always upset by it. That's why I, really, I have to say that. But I don't mean any, any harm by it. I didn't want to have children. I promised myself when I went out that I will not bring any children to this world.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I got pregnant. And I'm very happy now. But-- Rosette, back to Ravensbruck, what happened? How did you leave Ravensbruck?

speaker: interviewee
text: We left, all of us together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: To, to Neustadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened there?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Neustadt, we would linger around. There was not much to do because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there was-- they were liberating. As a matter of fact, the day that we heard that they liberated us, my girlfriend came over.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said-- she said to me-- she tapped me over my shoulder. And she said, you know what? We're liberated. Rosette, we liberated. And I went and said, so what? I cannot-- I don't know why. But that's how I felt. I felt that I really don't want to see the-- nobody, that I'd want to-- it took me a year to, to wear a purse, where-- to put mine IDs in.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came back-- even then, when we came back to France after two weeks being in a little house, my mother-- that lady that I talked about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and my friends, we were in a little house in Germany waiting to be transferred back to France.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were transferred back, on the plane-- on the-- on the train coming into France, they will come and ask, who is French? Who wasn't born in France, they didn't give money. Who, who was born in France, they got 100 francs or something. My girlfriend, Marie-Claude, came over to me because it hurt me. I said, even now? They're making a difference, even now? So she came over. And she split the money with me. She said, here. You're going to have money to buy bread. We didn't know what's waiting for us. So even then-- maybe that's why I was so-- I didn't believe in people. But I have to say that I have many, many good friends. And there are some good people. What happened the day you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: The day we were liberated--

speaker: interviewer
text: What day was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: May the 2nd.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? We were in barracks. And all of a sudden, we heard noises. Everybody got up. And everybody was screaming. And everybody ran out. In the morning, the Russian came, and later on, the American. I was just-- I was just keeping with my mother. I just-- nothing mattered to me. But they did it for me. I mean, they took me. We were very close. And we all went into a little house where the two ladies, the two mothers, took care of the youngest. And they would provide some food. And then they would cook. And I have to say, the day my mother got liberated, she was not the same because she was afraid to be-- to be-- have a slap. Since that girl slapped her, oh, she was so frightened to get-- that she always walked away. She wouldn't-- But that day, the day that she got liberated, she kind of-- she, she came to life. She took over. And they were cooking. And she was so busy with us, so busy. And the French people took us to, to Paris. And my mother lived with me. And she took care of everything. And my sister came to, to Paris from Poland. We, of course, we start inquiring about all our families. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] our apartment was occupied. And we found a very nice young man that lent us an apartment with cold water on the flat, of course. But we were very happy to be there. And also, when we came, we were-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were greeted by some people. They took us to Hotel Lutetia, which was a beautiful hotel. There was food prepared for us. And, and we didn't have where to go. So we stayed two weeks in that hotel, till that young man offered that apartment. My mother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took care of everything, like I say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do we have much more time? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had news from my uncle, who was the only one survivor from my-- from mine father. [SIGHS] My father had a family of five brothers and two sisters. They were all married. Then, my youngest uncle only survive, who is the same age of-- that I am. My mother had a sister. And she had a family. Because my mother was an orphan since she was eight years old. So she had only one sister. And the sister had a family. Nobody survived. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm getting dizzy. [LAUGHS] [SIGHS] My-- we took some-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we took some cures in France. And so my mother start working for us. And I helped her. We worked together with some other-- I'm not feeling good. Can we stop for a while? So that means I was rushing. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: I like to bring back my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We had-- I-- when I was born, my grandmother died. And I was named after her. I never knew my grandmothers. I knew my grandfather. My father's father was a very good-looking man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Mordechai. He was not-- he was modern. He had worn a-- he had a chain, short clothing. He wear a gold chain. And everybody in town respected him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he live?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Lodz. He-- as a matter of fact, when he died, half of the town went to his funeral. I was seven then-- couldn't read. But I somehow figured it out. We had-- I had a grandfather, my mother's father, who wore long clothes. He was religious. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He lived with us. [SOBBING] He took care always of us. My father respected him a lot. He was very loving. Even the non-Jews used to call him the 'Clean Grandpa.' He had a short-- he had a white beard. He wear a Jewish hat. And he wear Jewish clothes. But he cared about everybody, not only about us. He cared about the children in the court, if they were dressed properly, if they were warm. I loved him a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Wolf. My grandson is named after him. [SOBBING] And I have to say thank you again.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened? My-- they died. They died. My grandfather, one died when he was 53. The other one, he was 67, I believe-- the one that lived with us. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] if I recollect my childhood, I always remember [SOBBING] a lot of love. We cared about each other. There was never hollering. My mother would want to slap-- give us a spanking. We would run away. We always had help in the house. And I guess, till we went to France, life was very sweet. In France, of course, was more difficult. My father had to work. He had to-- didn't know the language. So that was hard. And then, of course, the war made, made this very hard for him in thinking that he has a daughter and a family that is still in Poland. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He used to say to us, please, children, finish your food. [SOBBING] Who knows if your sister has anything to eat? Come on, finish your food.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was a poor eater. But during the, the war, I start eating, I think, for two. And we cared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they observe Shabbat in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We observe Shabbat. My father, even though that he was not religious, and he was smoking a lot-- but he will never light a cigarette on Shabbat. When it came to the candle-lighting, my father would put away the cigarettes and didn't take a cigarette back till Shabbat was over. Not because he was so religious-- it was for the respect of my grandfather and for the respect of-- just the respect of the family. I wish that, in America, [SOBBING] the families should stay together just as close that we stayed. Somehow, it seems to me that everything is just falling apart. My cousins-- my children don't know their cousins. We came to America to-- my husband came to family, to first cousins. They have other cousins. It's just-- it's not the same.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rosette, what happened the first few years after liberation in Paris? When did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] My husband came to see my sister. He heard that my sister came from Poland. And he knew my sister from Poland. He came to see her. And then, when he met me, of course, he didn't speak any French. So I went with him to the-- to the American Embassy because he said that he has family in America. And he started to make his papers. When we got married, he wanted to go to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's? When I met him, was-- in French, was [NON-ENGLISH]-- no, in Poland, was [NON-ENGLISH]. In French-- was Felix. Came to America, it became Ralph. [LAUGHS] But his name is Rafael. Yeah, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: We got married in 1947-- January 19, 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do after you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't do anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any skills.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lived on Avenue Jean-Jaures in, in Paris-- one bedroom, one, one dining room, small kitchen, and a little entrance hall. And my husband worked very hard. He start to work at 6:00 in the morning, came back at 10:00 in the evening. After one year, my son was born, Claude. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm very proud of him. I loved him as soon as I saw him. [LAUGHS] And when, when he was four years old, we left for America-- just because to save him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Paterson, New Jersey, a hole in the wall. I hated it from the beginning-- little houses. In the summer, I said, we're not going to-- we're not going to need to go for vacation because we used to go every year in France and in Poland. We used to own a house in Poland, you know. And I never, never spent any summer in a city. So-- but the summer came. It was so sticky. It was a valley. So my husband sent my, my son and me to the mountains, famous Catskills. And from then on, we used to go every summer for two months to the Catskills.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was your daughter born?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter was born in Paterson, New Jersey, 1956, September 30th, go back and forth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Cathy. She's named after my mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to California?

speaker: interviewee
text: To California? 1963. My husband came in, in December 1962. I came in March because I was-- we were selling the house-- 1963, in March with my little girl. My son came a little later because he was attending school. And he was sent to, to a special school for eight weeks. And he joined us after.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know why I survived. I, I survived-- yeah, I think, thanks to my mother, I survived. But there wasn't such a thing that one survived or the other one didn't. I think it was just like a lottery, without buying the ticket. It just-- it just happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are your children married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do they have children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not both of them. My son has two children. One is Avi. He's 13. He just was bar mitzvah. The little girl is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the little girl is seven.
